title: China’s export surge + Huawei’s Tau Scaling Law + Sino-German trade
gist: Beijing is countering the EU's hardening stance on Chinese overcapacity with a narrative that Europe's export woes are self-inflicted competitiveness failures, even as Brussels assembles a cross-sectoral policy toolkit (Buy European, supplier blacklists, diversification and solidarity instruments) ahead of a next overcapacity wave signaled by China's next Five-Year Plan.

insights.claim: Beijing is actively promoting a narrative that China's flood of exports into Europe reflects European competitiveness failures rather than a threat from Chinese subsidized overproduction.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Watch for this framing to blunt EU political will for anti-China measures; it shifts blame from Chinese subsidies to European reform failure.
insights.quote: China’s flood of exports is more an issue of European competitiveness than a threat from Chinese overproduction.
insights.locator: Chinese experts claim Europe’s own problems

insights.claim: Chinese experts argue that if the EU responds to China's export surge with restrictions, it risks inflicting greater damage on itself than on China.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: This is a deterrence message aimed at raising the perceived cost of EU defensive measures before they consolidate.
insights.quote: risks inflicting greater damage on itself than on China
insights.locator: The central message emerging from Chinese experts

insights.claim: China's counter-narrative is a deliberate attempt to challenge the EU's diagnosis of the trade problem before it hardens into a durable policy consensus and coordinated action.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Timing signal: Beijing sees a closing window to disrupt EU consensus-building on China policy.
insights.locator: This assessment appears to form the core

themes.theme: EU trade grievances and hard numbers

insights.claim: The EU runs a goods trade deficit with China of roughly EUR 1 billion per day.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale driving EU political pressure for trade defense instruments.
insights.quote: a daily goods trade deficit with China of around EUR 1 billion
insights.locator: They point to a daily goods trade deficit

insights.claim: The EU is losing roughly 500 manufacturing jobs per day across the bloc, concentrated in sectors central to the green and digital transitions.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Job-loss framing gives EU leaders political cover for protectionist industrial policy in green and digital sectors.
insights.quote: the loss of roughly 500 manufacturing jobs per day across the EU
insights.locator: They point to a daily goods trade deficit

insights.claim: Volkswagen recently announced cost-saving measures that could lead to 100,000 job cuts in Europe.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A concrete corporate data point tying Chinese competition to European auto-sector retrenchment.
insights.quote: cost-saving measures that could lead to 100,000 job cuts in Europe

themes.theme: EU systemic policy toolkit against China

insights.claim: The EU's proposed Industrial Accelerator Act (March 2026) would add 'Buy European' clauses and impose conditions on foreign direct investment.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Buy European procurement rules would directly restrict Chinese market access; watch legislative progress.
insights.locator: Some of the key discussed actions include

insights.claim: An update to the EU Cybersecurity Act (proposed January 2026) would create a pan-EU high-risk supplier list limiting access to the European single market.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A supplier blacklist mechanism could exclude Chinese vendors (e.g. telecom/tech) EU-wide, a material market-access risk.
insights.locator: Some of the key discussed actions include

insights.claim: The EU is developing a diversification instrument to impose limits on single-supplier dependence and a solidarity instrument to spread the costs of Beijing's retaliation and coercion across member states.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A cost-sharing mechanism would reduce Beijing's ability to pick off individual member states through targeted coercion.
insights.locator: Some of the key discussed actions include

insights.claim: A next wave of Chinese industrial overcapacity is already heralded by the priority areas specified in China's next Five-Year Plan.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Forward signal: identify the Five-Year Plan priority sectors to anticipate where the next export glut and EU-China friction will land.
insights.locator: They could also help Europe prepare
